system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 8: The Diatonic Cycle of 4ths in Major Keys

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 8 — The Diatonic Cycle of 4ths in Major Keys

_Pages 49-58._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Cycle of 4ths definition

> The harmonic formula I IV vii? iii vi ii V 1 IV vii® iii vi etc. has been very popular for a long time. It is often
> teferred to as the CYCLE OF 4ths (or 5ths) meaning each chord root is a 41h higher (or 5th lower) than the previous
> chord root. Example: In the key of C, using 7th chords, the diatonic cycle of 4ths is C# F# Bm765 Em? Am7 Dm7
> G7 C+ F% etc, I IV. vii? iii vi ii
>
> ¥ 1 IV

**Significance (p. 49).** Foundational definition of the diatonic cycle of 4ths.

## Cycle root motion rule

> Looking at the root names of the chords, you will see that F is a 4th above C, B is a 4th (#4th) above F, E is a 4th
> above B, and so on.

**Significance (p. 49).** Teaches the interval rule that defines cycle motion.

## Cycle start chord flexibility

> Notice that iii vi ii V, with which you are already familiar, is contained in the cycle of 4ths. Also you should
> know that it is cammon for the cycle to begin on other chords than the I; for instance, you might encounter the cycle
> in a song in the following form: IV vii®° iii viii Vl. No matter which chord it starts on, as long as each chord is a 4th
> higher in the key, it is still the diatonic cycle of 4ths.

**Significance (p. 49).** Cycle is defined by interval motion, not starting point.

## Sequence pattern defined

> Most cycles of 4ihs use a SEQUENCE pattern (a sequence is an exact repetition of a musical idea but using
> different pitches. Example: way oF CG:

**Significance (p. 49).** Introduces sequence as the device organizing cycle-of-4ths patterns.

## Two-chord sequence unit

> 3 3 2z Notice that the sequence pattern used here involves units of two chords; C# FF,
> Ba? Em7, Am7 Dm7, G7 C+, F? B@7, etc. (the string transference slightly changed the
> pattern at the end, but unfortunately the physical limits of the instrument necessitate
> this type of thing sometimes, if you wish lo kecp a pattern going a little longer).

**Significance (p. 49).** Two-chord sequence unit plus honest acknowledgment of guitar physical constraints.

## Good I-to-IV connection method

> One trick to creating a good cycle of 4ths pattern is to get a good connection of the first two chords, and then
> carry the pattern down. This is where your knowledge of chord scales comes in. IMPORTANT: A cycle of 4ths
> pattern as illustrated above, can also be viewed as two chord scales descending, while they alternate with each other.

**Significance (p. 49).** Method's central technique: reduce cycle to one good I-IV plus two alternating descending chord scales.

## Two alternating chord scales

> Try playing just the first, third and fifth diagrams above; now play just the sccond, fourth and sixth diagrams.
>
> : The first, third and fifth diagrams belong to one chord scale and the second, fourth and sixth diagrams belong to
> another.

**Significance (p. 49).** Operationalizes the two-alternating-chord-scales concept.

## Cycle fingering and chord scale application

> Again notice that once the initial good connection of I and IV is made, it is just a matter of figuring out the
> descending chord scales from both chords and alternating them. The fingering on the G#° and F#m chords near the
> beginning was also changed to make the playing a little easier (don’t laugh, in the long run these fingerings will be
> easier; likewise the fingering on the C#m and Bm near the end).

**Significance (p. 50).** Reinforces procedure and establishes that fingerings may be altered for playability.

## Constructing cycles from IV vii°

> Only the I and IV chords will be given in this list, but you should make them into cycle of
> 4th patterns (if you are going to work with them) by figuring out the chord scales, most of which you already know if
> you have done your homework. Occasionally the IV and vii? chords will be given instead of the I and IV, for the sake
> of variety. But the procedure of figuring out the cycle by the two alternating chord scales is still the same, whether
> you start from IV vii® or I IV.

**Significance (p. 50).** Procedural rule for using the chord lists.

## Omitting/changing notes for physical limits

> Sometimes you will have to leave 2 note out of a chord (or change a note), due to the physical problem.

**Significance (p. 50).** Permission to modify voicings due to guitar limits.

## Practicing cycles across full neck

> To really know the whole instrument, you should practice each cycle in all octaves on each string set. For
> example, if you are given Ap De the first cycle pattern you will come up with will be as follows:

**Significance (p. 50).** Practice prescription for complete neck mastery.

## Thinking chord names while playing

> REMINDER — When you play these cycles, or any
> . : . progressions, for that matter),
> 7 4— This chord will be discussed later. NHINK ia Haraae Nemevale snd
> CHORD NAMES while you play: in the long
> run, they will sink in better with this technique.

**Significance (p. 49).** Pedagogical rule: think chord names while playing.

## Avoiding monotony; combining cycles

> Finally, one reminder: The cycle of 4ths is a beautiful harmonic tool, but it can be monotonous if overused, so
> try combining it eventually, with other ideas. You will be learning more about this in later volumes of this series, a8
> mentioned, but there is no harm in experimenting right away on your own. Even just combining different cycle
> patterns might be fun for now.

**Significance (p. 51).** Aesthetic rule: avoid overuse; invitation to experiment.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
